era_context: The Carolingian Empire is fragmenting following the death of Louis the Pious (814–840). His sons are contesting the division of territories, leading to civil war and the eventual Treaty of Verdun (843). In the Islamic world, the Abbasid Caliphate under al-Mu'tasim is consolidating power and expanding the use of slave soldiers (ghulams). In East Asia, the Tang Dynasty is in decline; the Huang Chao Rebellion and regional fragmentation are beginning. In Scandinavia, Viking raids continue to intensify across Western Europe.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records for this specific year)

events.title: Death of Louis the Pious; Carolingian succession crisis begins
events.description: Louis the Pious, son of Charlemagne and emperor of the Franks, dies on 20 June 840. His death triggers an immediate succession dispute among his three surviving sons: Lothair I, Louis the German, and Charles the Bald. Each claims overlapping territories, leading to civil war that will dominate the next three years. This event marks the effective end of Charlemagne's unified empire and the beginning of the fragmentation that will produce the medieval kingdoms of France, Germany, and Italy.
key_figures: Lothair I (eldest son, claims imperial title)

sources.name: Vita Hludowici Imperatoris (Life of Louis the Pious) by Thegan
Einhard's continuation and later Frankish annals

events.certainty_note: Death date of 20 June 840 is well-attested in contemporary Frankish chronicles. The succession crisis is documented in near-contemporary sources.
cross_references: Follows from 814-001 (Charlemagne's death and Louis's accession)

events.title: Al-Mu'tasim becomes Abbasid Caliph
events.description: Al-Mu'tasim bi-'llah (r. 833–842) is now firmly established as the eighth Abbasid Caliph, having succeeded his father al-Ma'mun in 833. By 840, he has consolidated his authority and is expanding the use of slave soldiers (ghulams and Mamluks) to reduce dependence on Arab tribal armies. He is also known for his patronage of the House of Wisdom and continued theological disputes over the nature of the Qur'an (the Mihna, or inquisition). His reign represents a shift toward military autocracy and the militarization of the caliphate.

sources.name: Tabari's History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk)
Abbasid administrative records and coins (numismatic evidence)

events.certainty_note: Al-Mu'tasim's reign dates (833–842) are well-established in Islamic historiography. His policies and military reforms are documented in later chronicles based on contemporary records.
cross_references: Leads to 847-001 (Anatolian campaigns against Byzantine Empire)

events.title: Viking raid on Rouen and Seine valley intensifies
events.description: Norse raiders intensify attacks on the Seine valley and Rouen in northern Francia, exploiting the Frankish succession crisis. These raids are part of the broader Viking expansion that accelerates during the 830s–840s. The weakness of Frankish central authority following Louis the Pious's death makes coordinated defense difficult. These raids presage the eventual establishment of Normandy as a Viking settlement zone by the Treaty of Saint-Clair-sur-Epte (911).
key_figures: Norse raiders (names largely unrecorded in contemporary sources)
events.certainty_note: Viking raids on the Seine are documented in Frankish annals for the 830s–840s period, though specific dates for individual raids are sometimes approximate. The year 840 is consistent with the documented escalation pattern.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Louis the Pious's death as a 'watershed' moment has been somewhat revised by modern scholarship. Some historians argue that the fragmentation of the Carolingian Empire was already underway during his reign due to regional power consolidation, and that the succession crisis merely accelerated an existing process rather than causing it. Additionally, the notion that the Frankish Empire 'fell apart' in 840 is an oversimplification; Lothair I maintained a claim to imperial authority, and the empire's institutional structures persisted in modified form.
historiographic_note: The year 840 is well-documented for Western Europe and the Islamic world, but documentation is heavily weighted toward political and military events recorded by clerical chroniclers and court historians. The Frankish annals are our primary source, and they reflect the perspective of the Frankish ecclesiastical establishment. For the Islamic world, sources are later compilations (Tabari, al-Mas'udi) based on earlier records. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Asia are absent from contemporary written records for this year. The 'Frankish perspective' dominates surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: Louis's death directly precipitated the civil war culminating in the Battle of Fontenoy
The succession crisis was resolved by the Treaty of Verdun in 843
Viking raids of the 840s were part of the broader Norse expansion that led to the settlement of Normandy
